content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: You ___ touch the paintings in the museum — there are signs everywhere saying it is forbidden.
distractor_rationale: don't have to means there is no obligation, but here touching is forbidden, not merely unnecessary
should expresses advice, not a strict prohibition backed by museum rules
must expresses obligation to do something, not a prohibition against doing it

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The entry to the park is free, so visitors ___ pay anything at the gate.
distractor_rationale: mustn't means it is forbidden to pay, which is nonsensical — paying is simply not required
shouldn't means it is inadvisable to pay, which contradicts the neutral fact that entry is free
must not expresses prohibition, but the sentence states there is no requirement, not that payment is banned

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: All passengers ___ wear a seatbelt while the car is moving — it is the law.
distractor_rationale: should expresses advice or a weaker recommendation, not a legal requirement
don't have to means there is no obligation, which directly contradicts the legal requirement stated
must to is grammatically incorrect — must is followed directly by the base verb without to

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: You look really tired — you ___ go to bed earlier on weeknights so you feel better at work.
distractor_rationale: must implies a strong external obligation or rule, but the speaker is giving personal advice based on concern, not imposing a rule
mustn't expresses prohibition, which is the opposite of the intended meaning of recommending an action
don't have to means there is no necessity to go to bed earlier, which contradicts the advice being given

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: When Sara was a student, she ___ hand in all her assignments by Friday or her grade would be affected.
distractor_rationale: must cannot be used to express obligation in the past; it has no past tense form for this meaning
musted is not a word in English; must has no simple past inflection
has to is the present tense form and cannot refer to a completed past routine

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The uniform is optional at this school, so students ___ wear it if they prefer their own clothes.
distractor_rationale: mustn't means wearing the uniform is forbidden, which contradicts the sentence — it is simply not required
ought not to means it is inadvisable to wear the uniform, which also contradicts the neutral statement about choice
must not expresses prohibition, not the absence of obligation that the sentence clearly communicates

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Employees ___ share their computer passwords with anyone, as it is a serious security risk.
distractor_rationale: don't have to means sharing is not necessary, but the sentence describes a prohibition due to a security risk
should expresses mild advice, whereas the security context demands a strong prohibition
have to means employees are obligated to share passwords, which is the exact opposite of the intended meaning

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The doctor said that Mark ___ avoid salty food and exercise at least three times a week to lower his blood pressure.
distractor_rationale: must implies a strong external rule or the speaker's own authority, whereas a doctor's reported advice is better expressed with ought to or should
had to refers to a completed past obligation, but the advice applies to Mark's ongoing behaviour from now on
doesn't have to means there is no necessity to avoid salty food, which directly contradicts the doctor's recommendation

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Because the fire alarm went off during the night, all the guests ___ leave their rooms immediately and gather in the car park.
distractor_rationale: must cannot express a completed past obligation with a specific past trigger; had to is required for past necessity
should have implies the guests failed to leave but ought to have done so, contradicting the sentence which states they did leave
were having to is not a standard English construction; have to does not typically form the past continuous in this way

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The contract clearly states that neither party ___ disclose the terms of the agreement to outside organisations without written consent.
distractor_rationale: should expresses a recommendation, but a formal legal contract uses must to impose a binding obligation
doesn't have to means disclosure is not required, which is the opposite of the prohibition the contract imposes
ought to expresses a weaker moral or advisory obligation and is inappropriate for the binding language of a formal contract

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: You ___ pay to enter the park — it is completely free for everyone.
distractor_rationale: mustn't means it is forbidden, but the sentence says it is free — there is no prohibition, just no requirement
must expresses strong obligation, which directly contradicts the fact that entry is free
should expresses advice or recommendation, but the sentence states a factual absence of any requirement

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Children ___ touch the hot oven — it can burn them badly.
distractor_rationale: don't have to means there is no obligation, but the sentence describes a dangerous prohibition, not a lack of necessity
should expresses mild advice, which is too weak for a serious safety rule about burning danger
must expresses positive obligation to do something, but the sentence requires a prohibition against touching

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If you feel tired after lunch, you ___ take a short rest before your afternoon meeting.
distractor_rationale: must implies a strict legal or external obligation, but the context is personal advice based on feeling tired, not a formal rule
mustn't expresses prohibition, which is the opposite of what is intended — the speaker is recommending rest, not forbidding it
don't have to states there is no obligation, but the speaker is actively giving advice, not removing a requirement

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: When Mark arrived late at the office last Monday, he ___ explain to his manager why he had missed the morning briefing.
distractor_rationale: must cannot be used to express obligation in a finished past situation — it has no past tense form for this meaning
musted is not a real English word — must has no simple past inflection and learners who add -ed are applying a regular verb rule incorrectly
should have expresses past criticism or regret about something that did not happen, but the sentence describes an obligation that was actually carried out

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Passengers ___ show a valid ticket before boarding the train, or they will be fined by the inspector.
distractor_rationale: should expresses advice or recommendation, which is too weak for an enforced rule backed by a financial penalty
don't have to means there is no obligation, which directly contradicts the fine mentioned in the second clause
ought to also expresses moral advice or recommendation and, like should, is too weak to convey an officially enforced regulation with a stated legal consequence

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: You ___ touch the paintings in the museum — it is not allowed.
distractor_rationale: don't have to means it is not necessary, not that it is forbidden — the context makes clear it is prohibited
should expresses advice or weak recommendation, not a strict prohibition
must expresses obligation to do something, not a prohibition against doing it

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The entrance to the park is free, so visitors ___ pay to get in.
distractor_rationale: mustn't means it is forbidden, but paying is simply not required — there is no prohibition against paying
shouldn't expresses that something is inadvisable, which does not fit a factual statement about a free entrance
must not is a prohibition, but the sentence states the entrance is free, meaning payment is simply unnecessary

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: All drivers ___ wear a seatbelt — it is the law in this country.
distractor_rationale: should expresses advice or a weaker recommendation, but the sentence states this is a legal requirement
must to is grammatically incorrect — must is a bare modal and is never followed by to
are must is not a grammatical construction in English

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Maria has a bad cough, so she ___ see a doctor before it gets worse.
distractor_rationale: mustn't expresses prohibition, but the sentence is giving health advice, not forbidding an action
don't have to means there is no obligation, which contradicts the advice implied by 'before it gets worse'
musted is not a word — must has no past tense inflection and cannot take -ed

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The conference starts at nine, so Tom ___ arrive by eight-thirty to set up the equipment.
distractor_rationale: don't have to means there is no obligation, which contradicts the practical necessity of setting up before the conference
mustn't means it is forbidden to arrive by eight-thirty, which makes no sense in context
ought is incomplete — the correct form is ought to, and even then it expresses weaker advice rather than the strong practical necessity shown here

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: You ___ to finish the report today — the deadline is next Friday, so there is plenty of time.
distractor_rationale: mustn't means finishing the report is forbidden, which contradicts the idea that there is simply extra time available
shouldn't means finishing today is inadvisable, but the sentence only says it is not necessary — there is nothing wrong with finishing early
must not also expresses prohibition, making it wrong for the same reason as mustn't

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Children under twelve ___ use the gym equipment without an adult present, according to the safety rules.
distractor_rationale: don't have to means it is not necessary, but the sentence refers to a safety rule that prohibits unsupervised use
ought to expresses a recommendation to do something, which is the opposite of what the safety rule requires
should expresses advice, not the strict prohibition that a formal safety rule implies

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: When James was a student, he ___ share a room with two other people because the private rooms were fully booked.
distractor_rationale: must cannot be used to express past obligation — it has no past tense form and must be replaced by had to in past contexts
musted is not a valid English word — must cannot be inflected with -ed to form a past tense
has to is present tense and cannot refer to a completed past situation indicated by 'when James was a student'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The hotel swimming pool closes at ten o'clock, so guests ___ swim after that time under any circumstances.
distractor_rationale: don't have to means swimming after ten is optional rather than forbidden, which contradicts 'under any circumstances'
hadn't to is not a standard English construction — the negative past of have to is didn't have to
ought not is grammatically incomplete and too weak — the correct form is ought not to, and even then it expresses advice rather than a firm rule

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Although the training session is recommended by the manager, employees ___ attend if they have a prior commitment that day.
distractor_rationale: mustn't means attending is forbidden, which directly contradicts the fact that the manager recommends the session
must expresses strong obligation, but the sentence explicitly states attendance is only recommended, not required
ought to expresses a recommendation to attend, which is the opposite meaning — the sentence says attendance is not obligatory

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: You must to wear a uniform at this school every day.
content.error_sentence: You must to wear a uniform at this school every day.
content.corrected_sentence: You must wear a uniform at this school every day.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Children mustn't to run near the swimming pool because it is dangerous.
content.error_sentence: Children mustn't to run near the swimming pool because it is dangerous.
content.corrected_sentence: Children mustn't run near the swimming pool because it is dangerous.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The exam starts at nine, so you don't have to be late or they won't let you in.
content.error_sentence: The exam starts at nine, so you don't have to be late or they won't let you in.
content.corrected_sentence: The exam starts at nine, so you mustn't be late or they won't let you in.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The meeting is optional, so your colleagues mustn't attend if they have other work to finish.
content.error_sentence: The meeting is optional, so your colleagues mustn't attend if they have other work to finish.
content.corrected_sentence: The meeting is optional, so your colleagues don't have to attend if they have other work to finish.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: When Sara was a student, she must study for several hours every evening to keep up with her classes.
content.error_sentence: When Sara was a student, she must study for several hours every evening to keep up with her classes.
content.corrected_sentence: When Sara was a student, she had to study for several hours every evening to keep up with her classes.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Passengers mustn't show their boarding pass at the gate — the staff scan it automatically from your phone.
content.error_sentence: Passengers mustn't show their boarding pass at the gate — the staff scan it automatically from your phone.
content.corrected_sentence: Passengers don't have to show their boarding pass at the gate — the staff scan it automatically from your phone.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: After the pipe burst last winter, the whole building had to don't use the water for almost two days.
content.error_sentence: After the pipe burst last winter, the whole building had to don't use the water for almost two days.
content.corrected_sentence: After the pipe burst last winter, the whole building couldn't use the water for almost two days.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Drivers should to carry their licence at all times under the road safety regulations in this area.
content.error_sentence: Drivers should to carry their licence at all times under the road safety regulations in this area.
content.corrected_sentence: Drivers should carry their licence at all times under the road safety regulations in this area.
